settings.title: Why Your Bread Goes Stale and How to Slow It Down
settings.excerpt: The fridge is the enemy, and a paper bag is doing more than you think.
settings.body:
A question we hear at the counter every week is how to keep a loaf fresh for longer. A few habits make a real difference.

Keep it out of the fridge. Cold air actually speeds up staling. A bread box or paper bag on the counter is better.

Cut from the middle. Slice from the center and press the two halves together cut-side in. The crust protects the crumb.

What is actually happening to the loaf

Stale is not the same as dry, even though they feel similar. As bread cools and sits, the starches in the crumb slowly recrystallise and firm up, and the fridge speeds that process rather than slowing it. That is why a loaf left in the fridge overnight feels worse than one left on the counter. Plastic wrap traps moisture against the crust and turns it soft and leathery, which is the opposite of what you want, so paper is the better choice for a crusty loaf.

Knowing that, the fixes mostly come down to controlling moisture and temperature without reaching for the cold.

Quick habits that help

  • Store cut-side down so the crumb stays sealed by the crust.
  • Use paper rather than plastic for anything with a real crust.
  • Keep the loaf at room temperature, never in the fridge.

If you cannot finish a loaf in two days, slice it and freeze it. A few seconds in the toaster brings it most of the way back, far closer to fresh than a fridge ever will.
